expectations.assertion: Value objects (Money, Quantity) are separate classes with value equality, not raw primitives
expectations.evidence: Money.cs extends ValueObject base and implements GetEqualityComponents() yielding Amount+Currency. Quantity.cs extends ValueObject and yields Value. ValueObject base class overrides Equals/GetHashCode using SequenceEqual on components. Tests confirm equality semantics: Equality_SameAmountAndCurrency_AreEqual, Equality_DifferentAmount_AreNotEqual, Equality_SameValue_AreEqual, etc. Both are proper classes, not raw decimal/int.

expectations.assertion: Domain entities have private setters — no public property setters on Order or OrderLineItem
expectations.evidence: Order.cs: 'public Guid CustomerId { get; private set; }', 'public OrderStatus Status { get; private set; }'. OrderLineItem.cs: 'public Guid ProductId { get; private set; }', 'public Quantity Quantity { get; private set; } = null!', 'public Money UnitPrice { get; private set; } = null!'. No public setters on any property. LineItems exposed as IReadOnlyList. TotalAmount and LineTotal are computed properties with no setter at all.

expectations.assertion: Entities use private constructors with static factory methods returning Result<T>
expectations.evidence: Order: private Order() and private Order(Guid id, Guid customerId); public static Result<Order> Create(...). OrderLineItem: private OrderLineItem() and private OrderLineItem(Guid id, ...); public static Result<OrderLineItem> Create(...). Money and Quantity follow the same pattern. All constructors are private.

expectations.assertion: Result<T> pattern is used for operations that can fail — no throwing exceptions for business rule violations
expectations.evidence: Result.cs defines Result and Result<T> with IsSuccess/IsFailure/Error. All fallible operations return Result types: Order.Create, Order.AddLineItem, Order.Confirm, OrderLineItem.Create, Money.Create, Money.Add, Quantity.Create. No throw statements exist in any domain class for business rule violations. Error messages are returned via Result.Failure(...).

expectations.assertion: Order aggregate enforces the $10,000 maximum total invariant
expectations.evidence: Order.cs declares 'private const decimal MaxOrderValue = 10_000m'. Checked in Create() when first item's lineTotal > MaxOrderValue, and in AddLineItem() when currentTotal + newLineTotal > MaxOrderValue. Three tests cover this: AddLineItem_ExceedingMaxOrderValue_ReturnsFailure (5000+5001), Create_WithLineItemExceedingMaxOrderValue_ReturnsFailure (10001), AddLineItem_ExactlyAtMaxOrderValue_Succeeds (5000+5000=10000 allowed). Error message includes '10,000'.

expectations.assertion: Order aggregate prevents adding items after confirmation
expectations.evidence: Order.AddLineItem(): 'if (Status == OrderStatus.Confirmed) return Result.Failure("Cannot add items to a confirmed order")'. Test AddLineItem_WhenConfirmed_ReturnsFailure confirms the order then tries AddLineItem and asserts IsFailure with error containing 'confirmed'.

expectations.assertion: Order requires at least one line item (enforced at creation or confirmation)
expectations.evidence: Order.Create() signature requires firstProductId, firstQuantity, firstUnitPrice as mandatory parameters — it is impossible to create an Order without providing a first line item. The method creates and adds the OrderLineItem before returning success. Test Create_WithValidCustomerIdAndLineItem_ReturnsSuccess asserts Assert.Single(result.Value.LineItems). Note: there is no separate 'zero items' failure path because the API design makes it structurally impossible to create an empty order.

expectations.assertion: xUnit tests exist covering all 5 business rules
expectations.evidence: 38 total xUnit tests across 4 files. Business rules covered: (1) at least one line item - Create_WithValidCustomerIdAndLineItem_ReturnsSuccess; (2) Quantity must be positive - Theory Create_WithZeroOrNegative_ReturnsFailure; (3) UnitPrice non-negative - Create_WithNegativeAmount_ReturnsFailure; (4) Total <= $10,000 - 3 tests (exceed, exact, at-creation); (5) Confirmed order rejects items - AddLineItem_WhenConfirmed_ReturnsFailure. All rules covered. Transcript confirms 38/38 passed.

expectations.assertion: Tests are behavioral — they test business outcomes, not just getters/setters
expectations.evidence: Tests verify business outcomes: Create_SetsStatusToPending checks status after creation; Create_RaisesOrderCreatedDomainEvent checks domain event was raised; AddLineItem_ExceedingMaxOrderValue_ReturnsFailure verifies invariant enforcement; Confirm_RaisesOrderConfirmedDomainEvent verifies event lifecycle; TotalAmount_WithMultipleLineItems_ReturnsSumOfLineTotals tests computed business logic (10*2 + 15*3 = 65). Tests exercise real domain behavior rather than trivially reading/writing properties.

expectations.assertion: Domain events are raised for significant state changes (order created, confirmed)
expectations.evidence: OrderCreatedEvent.cs: 'public record OrderCreatedEvent(Guid OrderId, Guid CustomerId, DateTime OccurredAt) : IDomainEvent'. OrderConfirmedEvent.cs: 'public record OrderConfirmedEvent(Guid OrderId, DateTime OccurredAt) : IDomainEvent'. Order.Create() calls 'order.AddDomainEvent(new OrderCreatedEvent(...))'. Order.Confirm() calls 'AddDomainEvent(new OrderConfirmedEvent(...))'. AggregateRoot exposes DomainEvents as IReadOnlyList with ClearDomainEvents(). Tests Create_RaisesOrderCreatedDomainEvent and Confirm_RaisesOrderConfirmedDomainEvent verify Assert.Single(order.DomainEvents).

claims.assessment: PLAUSIBLE — transcript explicitly states 'Passed! - Failed: 0, Passed: 38, Skipped: 0, Total: 38' and 'Build succeeded. 0 Warning(s), 0 Error(s)'. Code structure is consistent and would compile correctly.
PLAUSIBLE — transcript documents each cycle with explicit RED (build failure message), GREEN (test counts), REFACTOR steps. Build failure messages are specific and match the actual code structure.

claims.claim: Nullable warnings fixed by adding = null! to EF Core constructor properties
claims.assessment: CONFIRMED — OrderLineItem.cs lines 9-10 show 'public Quantity Quantity { get; private set; } = null!' and 'public Money UnitPrice { get; private set; } = null!' exactly as described.

user_notes_summary: Implementation is a textbook tactical DDD example using the dotnet-tactical-ddd skill patterns. All five business rules are enforced at the domain layer. The 'at least one line item' invariant is enforced structurally (API requires first item at creation time) rather than via a runtime check, which is a stronger guarantee. The $10,000 max is checked in both Create() and AddLineItem() paths. Domain events are raised for both order creation and confirmation. All 38 tests pass with zero warnings.

eval_feedback.skill_quality: HIGH — dotnet-tactical-ddd skill produced correct Result<T>, ValueObject, Entity, AggregateRoot base classes that the implementer used directly without modification. Skill guidance on private constructors, factory methods, and domain events was followed precisely.
notable_strengths: Structural enforcement of 'at least one line item' — impossible to construct empty Order via the public API
Max order value checked in both Create() and AddLineItem() — no path to violate the invariant
ClearDomainEvents() used correctly in Confirm_RaisesOrderConfirmedDomainEvent test to isolate events
Theory attributes used for parameterized edge cases (invalid currencies, zero/negative quantities)
Boundary test: AddLineItem_ExactlyAtMaxOrderValue_Succeeds verifies the limit is non-exclusive
notable_weaknesses: No test verifying that Order has no line items after all removals (but no remove functionality exists, so N/A)
Confirm_AlreadyConfirmed_ReturnsFailure is a guard test but idempotent confirm is not a stated business rule — minor over-specification, not a deficiency
